speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four, December 21, 1995. I'm interviewing Ira Kaminsky. My name is Daniel Sedlis. So go ahead. You were talking about your assignment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was assigned to work in a factory which produced underwater mines. There were two types. I was assigned-- first it was the white powder. And there was also yellow powder, which they called pickrin. White powder was trotele, and the yellow powder was pickrin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trotele? T-R-O-T-E-L-E. Trotele.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the yellow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The yellow was pickrin. P-I-C-K-R-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did your work consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was four people was assigned to press-- to one press. Put it together in certain-- in certain way and press it. I was the presser. This was the hardest part and the most exhausting, but I was-- I was-- I was all right with that. I didn't mind as long as  I didn't have any problems with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we were producing. The problem was that at this-- at this-- these places where I was assigned, we were glued to the place. We couldn't even go away for, for a minute to relieve ourselves, because otherwise, we couldn't do the norm, you know? The quota that you had.

speaker: interviewee
text: The quota that we had to produce. And especially after being over there a month, people got exhausted. They didn't-- we didn't have enough food. We had only in the morning a little coffee. 12 o'clock, we had a little watery soup. And then, in the evening, we had a portion of bread. And with a diet like this, the people could not survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the other places in the other jobs, which they could move around in the camps, like my brother-- he was in transportation. He was working on the grenades, the big ones. From one place, empty shells to fill into the other place. So he was always on the move. And he-- these people, they could help themselves with some food, buy something, sell something, you know? And help yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't do that, not at all. Whatever they gave us, that's what we had to live on. And thanks to my brother, he did help me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he able to get you the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- there was one wagon. There were  four Jewish people that they-- that they had-- you know, they decided that they have to do their work. Nobody will do it for them. And they did their work. They didn't fool around. And they kept themselves pretty good. And one German saw the way they were working. And that German went to the kitchen, and he said, you have to give every day to these four people extra food, extra soup. And from that soup, my brother shared with me, and it helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you see your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I see what?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see your brother? Did you share a barrack--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- only in the-- in the-- after work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you slept in the same barrack with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Only the people that were working with us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the same factory, we slept-- we had a separate barrack. And my brother was in transportation. He slept in a different barrack. But he brought it, or he asked somebody to bring the soup to me. And that's why--

speaker: interviewer
text: He snuck it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Yes. He brought it-- during lunchtime, he gave it to somebody. Do me a favor. They-- they were allowed to move one place to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So-- OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not allowed to go anywhere. And they gave me. And it helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact at this point with your sister Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Sunday-- we had to-- I would-- I want-- I'd like to go to this to-- to take a bath, we had to go to barrack A. In barrack C, there was no place where to take a bath. So every Sunday, we used to-- it was about three, about three or four miles from barrack C to barrack A. And that's where my, my sister knew already that I came. And she prepared also a soup or something for me, you know? Over there it was not as bad as barrack C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women in barrack C, or only--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Woman was also in, in, in barrack C.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were women in barrack C.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But they were separate barracks--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and the men were separate-- barracks. As a matter of fact, women were working in the same place where I was. They were supplying the powder. It had to be weighed the exact amount, you know, and they were working on these things, you know. And the only one problem was the selections that we suffered immensely, terribly. They came-- the Germans came every week or every two weeks. They came usually after the people left to work. Whoever stayed home for any reason-- claimed that he was sick or he was injured at work-- and they took them out. And they took them to a certain place they called strzelnica. This was the firing place and testing place. and over there, they were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Strzelnica being a Polish word for the firing place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Firing place is a Polish word, strzelnica, yeah. The firing place and testing place. This was over there. They tested, ammunitions and if they, you know. And this was-- that people, you know, were scared, and everybody. It happened. Sometimes you were injured, or-- And people were sick with the typhoid. They couldn't stay home, and they couldn't walk even. So people were so nice and so supporting. They carried the men. Two men carried them because they had high fever, and they were so exhausted. For 13 days-- after the 13th day, they-- if he went through the crisis, then he survived. Some-- a lot of them died from the-- from the-- from-- from typhoid. And people were very supportive. And they used to carry them to work and do whatever they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have occasion to do this at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. We did. But when our place was much harder because four men were assigned to one press. Three men could not produce the quota. With four men-- it was hard for four men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But mostly they did at the transportation transport. They were working at the transport. You know, there were four or five people and one wagon. They could push the wagon, three or four, without one man. The other man were-- was-- was doing-- that sick man was doing put it on paper and shift. They were working the grenades, one on top of the other. So on top. So he did something, the sick man. But they saved a lot, a lot of men. One was working with my brother. And my brother, they used to take him every day to work. They assigned him, you know-- they put them and decide he shouldn't work hard because he couldn't work hard. And the Polish foreman that time caught him that he was-- they hide-- they were hiding him in a certain place, you know, until he would get well. And they caught him, and they beat him up so badly he died. He was weak from the typhoid, but they killed him all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you hear about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother. He was working with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find out about this at the camp? Or did you find out about this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was in our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that. And you found out about it at the same time that it happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, when this happened. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was communication between you, and you were able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to bring also one fact about my neighbor, Gavriel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Sure. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very strong man, a six footer, strong like a bull. And he had an infection on one finger. He was a very good worker. And he stayed home one day. They came, and they made a selection. And you know, the Germans right away, everybody out. Whoever didn't go to work, everybody out. So my brother was working that time at night. He said, don't go. Don't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what do you mean don't go? They're not going to do anything to me, men like me, strong and everything else, 'cause a-- 'cause a-- a finger, I've got a little infection on the finger. They wouldn't take me. They wouldn't kill me. What do you think? He didn't listen to my brother's warning, and he went out. And he told them, I'm strong and everything else. As soon as my finger will heal, I will go back to work. You didn't go to work, and you have to go. And they went and they killed him also. Gavriel lost his life. He was never seen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother reported this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never seen him again. They took him. [INAUDIBLE] They knew already where to take these sick people or whoever didn't go to work. I had once-- I had once in fact that I was scared I didn't go to work. I couldn't go to work. One guy-- two guys, as a matter of fact, were that time, they tried to escape from the-- from the camp. And one of them, he was from the same city, from Chmielnik, stole my shoes. I came, I went up, ready to go to work. I couldn't find my shoes. Without shoes, I couldn't go. It was in the winter time. They gave me some wooden shoes that they made that time. They called them Holland [NON-ENGLISH] from Holland. In Holland, they used to wear something like it, the farmers. And they gave me, and I went to work with these shoes. Holland [NON-ENGLISH]. Holland [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call them, the wooden shoes. I hated them. They were uncomfortable and everything else. After a while, my brother saw to it, over there. And they knew-- the commander also knew that they stole. And after about a couple of weeks, I got some shoes and I went back. And I was again normal. I could have lost my life that time to stay one day. I was very lucky. I never got sick, and I never wanted to stay [? and I ?] not to go to work. No matter what, I had to go to work. That's the way it was in barrack C.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there in barrack C?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about two years--

speaker: interviewer
text: About two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: --over there in barrack C. But I would like also-- I had once in a Sunday, which we were supposed to have off, they took us to clean, clean the machinery. And I went back in the back to clean-- in the back all the machines to clean. The, the boss from the hala 15 this was-- he was the-- the, the [NON-ENGLISH]. They called them. And he was over there. He happened to be over there,  and I didn't know that he was over there, otherwise I wouldn't go there. And he saw me. And I was doing some work and cleaning. And he started hitting me with a stick. My head was swollen.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was hitting your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hit in my head. And I didn't-- I don't know why. I didn't scream. I don't know. He was used to the guys, if he was hitting, they were screaming. I didn't scream. And this maybe bothered him. I stayed, and I didn't scream. After a while, he saw. He recognized me. The presses, all the presses, there was something wrong with the machinery. And I, being a presser, you know, for some times, I had an idea. And he was-- he was calling various machinists and various mechanics and everything, and they couldn't do a thing. And I, I showed him-- and he was present over there, that murder. And he [INAUDIBLE]. And he saw me, that I have an idea, even with the presses that are going very low. And they don't compress as they supposed to. And I still-- I didn't want to stay away, because if they-- if we didn't work, if we didn't have any work, they send us to various works which was even worse. This was bad, but we were used to it. We were not used to carry very heavy grenades like my brother. He was used to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- so we did something. I showed him that we can still work. We cannot-- I told him, we cannot produce the quota. But a certain amount, we can still produce. Since then, he knew that he beat me up so badly. He put me in charge of the-- of the production, of the quality of production.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had it much easier that time. I had to go around and check if the production-- I was-- I was responsible for the quality of production. If there was something wrong, he warned me, you're going to be responsible. And if something was wrong, they were accusing of sabotage. And I saw to it, well, it was my job. I checked and everything else if-- if the production is all right. Once it was-- when we used to go to work, a foreman from our division, a Polish man, came to the barracks, the camp, and he took us to work. He brought us to work. And that time, I became friendly with a Polish man. After a while, he saw that I am in charge of something and I am doing my job all right. So he, you know, and we were talking. He was the one who received the production. He took out with the wagon the production that we produced. And we became friendly.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was going from the barracks-- it was a night shift-- and he told me, did you hear what happened? The underground did some work, some sabotage work, for the Germans. And, and I saw-- noticed that he was very happy with that, because you have to be-- you had to be very careful. They had some collaborators, the Germans, which they were collaborating and working for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, I see the guy. He was a foreman over there. And he was very happy that the Germans had their-- had this, that the underground did do some operations against the Germans. And I saw his face. He was very, very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said I said to myself, should I or shouldn't I start? So I ask him, listen, if I'll tell you something, a word, would you know what it is? So I told him, if I'll say Miecz i Plug, which it means a sword and a plow, this was the name of the Polish underground publication, which I had told you before that I received and I read it always. And I got it from my friend, from my Polish friend. When he heard this, he just surprisingly looked at me. Where do you know that, he asked me. Well, I said, I know about it. But do you know that? He said, of course I know what it is. I saw that this man had also a contact with the Polish underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I've got something to tell you. And I would like-- if you don't want, tell me right away that you are not interested. I said to him, listen, you know what's going on here. We have to produce 15 boxes of 150 productions, pieces of mines in a box, about 2,200 something. And it's impossible. You see, the people, they don't get any food. From that food, they-- they can't produce. They're very weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any possibility-- you are receiving material, the production from us. Any possibility, instead 15 boxes that you have to get from, from each press, you should be satisfied with 14. Could you do this to me-- for me? It would be a very great thing. It could help all these people. When it comes to produce the last one, they are just exhausted, and they can't produce it. He said, Kaminsky, I'll take it on the consideration. I'll give you tomorrow an answer. And I, I said right away, I thought to myself that he has a connection with the underground. The following day he came. He said, I am considering to do that. Do you know how dangerous it is, what we are going to do? If they will find out, you will lose your life, and I will lose my life. I don't know if I lose my life or my job or something. But you for sure will use-- lose your life. This is sabotage.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am ready for it, I said, if you-- if you are ready for it. And then, for some times, we did produce not 15 boxes, but each press, 150 pieces of mines less. And the people they-- you know, they relaxed. And this was going on for some times. Once I--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans never found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never this. Never find out. Later on, I found out why he could do it. Then I said-- after a while I said, you know, the 13-- hala 13, the hall 13, that they are producing, the, the, the pickrin, the yellow stuff, over there it was Professor [? Ringel ?] in charge of the production like I was here in charge, Professor Ringel. And he-- I spoke to him. And he found out that we are doing less, 14 boxes instead 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, please, Kaminsky, find out if he will do it also for me. I-- I asked him the following day, would you do it for, for the hala-- for the hall 13 with pickrin, and work with Professor Ringel? He says, I'll try. I'll see what I can do. After a while, he did also with Professor Ringel from the hall 13. It was a bigger place. We had only three presses. They had five or six presses, twice as much. And it was some relief also for the people, for these yellow people from this. It was a tremendous thing for them especially, because they were exhausted people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after a while, something happened that we couldn't believe that it will come to it. A Jewish man went to the Germans, to the hala 15. And he reported what we are doing. I guess-- I was told later on-- later on, I found out how this man was OK. There was a Pollack by the name [? Kopetsky ?], who was a Polish man, who spoke German very well. In the beginning we thought maybe he's a German, a volksdeutsche we used to call him. But later on, I found out that this man was the leader, one of the leaders of the Polish underground. When the guy came in, the Jew, and he squealed that we are doing, we are supplying instead 15 boxes per, per press, and we are giving-- we are producing only 14, so the Polish man had to-- to-- to explain to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH].-- old man was that time the boss, the other one [INAUDIBLE]. And he didn't understand completely what happened. Anyway, he said to arrest Ira Kaminsky and Professor Ringel and put them-- and we will see what we're going to do with them because this is sabotage. Anyway, at that time, we were very short in people. And, you know, I was considered a good worker and Professor Ringel, also a good worker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what did he do? He took me away as the man who was responsible for the production and put me to the pickrin, to the yellow production. And Professor Ringel, who was in charge of production, also at the press. And this was that time the punishment. I was-- in the beginning, I was a candidate to be killed, to be shot, and Professor Ringel, the same thing. And instead, they put us both of us to work in the pickrin at the press. Thank god.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while-- after a while at that time, from Krakow, we received about 1,500 young people. And the-- and the commandant, the Jewish commandant from-- Isenberg was his name-- he was very, very upset about that squealing at me and Professor Ringel. And that man, for some reason, he didn't survive too long. After about a month, he died. So as soon-- they got new--

speaker: interviewer
text: The man who squealed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The squealer. And as soon as we received about 1,500 people from Krakow, from Plaszow, he took me out. Now you've got a chance to take-- to take you away from the punishment. Otherwise they might-- they will eventually-- if they're going to be short people again or something, they'll find out. They'll check what-- what it was. You're gonna work here in the barracks. And they took me out from the barracks-- from the pickrin, and I was working in the barracks. And this was a relief, great relief. And he saved my life that time too, but I didn't work anymore with the yellow stuff. I became altogether yellow. When I came and people saw me, I went to my sister and she saw me, she didn't recognize me. And this was the worst thing. This was a killer. People couldn't survive working a long time on the pickrin over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It could have been fatal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fatal. Pickrin was a strong chemical which it, it made you sick, you know? And it was-- when a German came into the place, he was always covered, covered his face. He shouldn't breathe in because it was a choking powder. And we used to stay for eight hours and work with it with that powder. And thank god. That's why people, when they saw me after the war, that I am still alive, they couldn't believe it. How do you survive the pickrin, working with pickrin? But I did. Thank god. I did survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other jobs at Skarzysko-Kamienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They took me in. And I was working that time in the barber shop in Werke C. And this was a relief. But I would like to bring up another thing. When I was working over there at the trotele, you know, I didn't mind working-- I was short, like I said. In the morning, they gave us only coffee. I said, if I will have a little-- a piece of bread with the coffee in the morning, I'm not going to be hungry. This, this might save me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what I did is, in the beginning, I was working in the barber shop, the barber. There was one single barber at that time. And he gave me-- I used to help him with 20 people with haircuts. And he gave me-- he collected two zloty, and he gave me one zloty. So I had 20 zloty. For the 20 zloty, I could go out and buy myself a bread, and to divide this bread every day for breakfast, I should have but whatever. And this was a saver also. [INAUDIBLE]. So Skarzysko-Kamienna, you had the ability to move around as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just-- I could move around at the working place. But in the barracks, Sunday was our day off. I could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could leave the barracks on Sunday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Within the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whom did you buy the bread from? From other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we could-- we could buy in the barracks, or sometimes Polish people brought it, and they made a few dollars on it, and we bought it from them. Once--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, I would like to bring out a [INAUDIBLE] this that I had contact with the Polish man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did something for me. And there were other Polish people which they were impossible, wild, sadists. So what I want to bring out, I had a 20 zloty that I was working Sunday, made Sunday. And I was working for one-- at that time, I had to-- I checked on two places, because one guy who was responsible for the-- for the production escaped. So they made me work on two places to check. From this place, it was one block from one hall to the other. I had to go to two places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one Pollack came to me, and he said, would you like to buy bread? I said, yes. Where is it? OK, come in over there into the neighboring hall. Over there, we have bread. OK. Come in in five minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In five minutes, I came. And I went in. And he said, I ask him how much. He said 20 zloty. I take out the 20 zloty, and I gave it to him. Meantime, another guy behind me, which I didn't know, threw a blanket on me. And all of a sudden, they started hitting me-- I don't know how many guys-- hitting me, and I fell down. They beat me up so badly. And they took the money. [INAUDIBLE] They took the blanket away, and they ran away. All right.
